title: Night on the Galactic Railroad
author: After Miyazawa Kenji
paragraphs: Giovanni was a quiet boy who lived at the edge of a small town in a house that leaned slightly to one side, as if it were tired. His father had gone away to sea long ago and had not come back. His mother was ill and spent most of her days in bed. After school, Giovanni worked at the printing shop, picking up tiny lead letters with aching fingers, earning just enough to buy milk for his mother. The other children sometimes teased him, but he never argued back. He just kept walking, eyes on the ground.

That evening the town was celebrating the Festival of Stars. Paper lanterns shaped like constellations hung from every doorway, and little boats carrying candles floated down the river, their reflections doubling the light. Children ran through the streets laughing, waving sparklers that left trails of gold in the dark. Giovanni delivered his bottle of milk, then climbed alone to the hill above the town. He sat in the grass and watched the festival from a distance, feeling as though he were looking at it all through a pane of glass.

His best friend was a boy called Campanella, the son of one of the wealthiest families in town. Campanella had gentle eyes and a quiet smile, and he never once looked down on Giovanni for being poor. But tonight, at the festival, Campanella was with the other children, and Giovanni did not feel brave enough to join them. He lay back in the grass and stared up at the sky. The Milky Way stretched overhead like a tremendous river of light, so bright he could almost hear it humming.

Then a blinding flash swept across the heavens, and Giovanni heard the long, clear whistle of a train. He blinked, and when he opened his eyes he was sitting on a velvet seat inside a railway carriage. Beyond the window spread an endless sky full of stars, and the whole compartment glowed with a soft silver light. He looked down. In his hand was a ticket printed in characters he had never seen before, shimmering faintly green, like a firefly resting on his palm.

Giovanni! called a familiar voice. He turned, and there was Campanella sitting right beside him, smiling as if they had planned to meet here all along. You are here too, Campanella said. Giovanni felt a wave of happiness so strong that all his loneliness melted away like snow in warm water. The two boys sat shoulder to shoulder, watching the starlight stream past the windows. The train rocked gently, like a boat sailing down a river made of light.

The first stop was the Northern Cross. A tremendous white cross stood in an open field, glowing as though carved from moonlight itself. Around its base, hundreds of white lilies bloomed, and on every petal a drop of dew held a tiny reflected galaxy. The air smelled sweet, like honey and rain together. Giovanni and Campanella pressed their faces to the glass, too amazed to blink, too happy to speak. The cross slowly receded behind them as the train moved on.

Next came the fields of pampas grass, stretching to every horizon. But this was no ordinary grass. Each plume was made of crystal, catching the starlight and scattering it into flecks of gold, silver, and pale blue. When the wind stirred the field, the crystal plumes chimed against one another, producing a sound like a thousand tiny bells ringing far away. Giovanni closed his eyes and listened. It was the most beautiful music he had ever heard, gentle enough to make you drowsy.

Look at the river, Campanella whispered, pointing out the window. The Milky Way flowed right alongside the tracks, its water as clear as glass. The riverbed was paved with smooth gemstones of every color, and schools of fish darted through the current, each one glowing softly, like a little swimming lantern. Now and then a bubble rose from the water, and when it reached the air it turned into a tiny star and drifted upward, joining the rest of the sky.

The train paused at the Swan Station. The doors opened and several passengers stepped aboard, their clothes dripping wet. A young teacher led the way, holding the hands of a small boy and a small girl. The teacher's face was calm and strangely peaceful. He told Giovanni that they had come from a great ship far out at sea. When the water rose, he had held the two children close, and then, somehow, they had found themselves here, on this train among the stars.

The little girl had bright, curious eyes. She gazed out the window and whispered, these stars are so beautiful, even lovelier than the phosphorescence in the ocean. The small boy leaned quietly against the teacher and slowly closed his eyes. Giovanni watched them and felt something tender stir inside his chest, a feeling he could not name. Campanella watched too, and his eyes seemed to hold a light that came from very far away, as if he were remembering something Giovanni could not see.

The train started again and passed through an orchard of apple trees. Every apple on every branch was golden and glowing, filling the orchard with a light as warm as afternoon sunshine. Campanella reached out through the window, and an apple dropped gently into his open hand. He broke it in two and gave half to Giovanni. Giovanni bit into it. The taste was so sweet it reminded him of the lullabies his mother used to sing when she was well, songs about the moon and sleeping birds.

Ahead, the Southern Cross came into view. A great cross even larger than the first blazed with warm golden light, like a lighthouse for the entire galaxy. Many passengers rose from their seats. The young teacher stood up and took the children by their hands. We have arrived, he said softly. The little girl turned back toward Giovanni and waved. Goodbye, she said, her voice as light as a breeze. Then they stepped off the train and walked into the golden glow.

Giovanni watched their figures grow transparent in the light, as though they were dissolving into it, becoming part of the radiance itself. A small ache bloomed in his chest. Campanella reached over and took his hand. Giovanni, he said quietly, what do you think true happiness is? I think maybe it is this: if you can make someone else happy, then wherever you go, that is a happy place. His voice was soft and sure, like someone who had thought about this for a long time.

The train entered a region of absolute darkness. This was the Coalsack, a place in the Milky Way where no stars shone at all. The silence was so deep it felt as if the entire universe were holding its breath. But Giovanni was not afraid, because Campanella's hand was still warm in his. In that perfect darkness he felt, strangely, more peaceful than he had all night, as though nothing could hurt him as long as they were together.

The darkness slowly thinned, and starlight returned to the windows. But when Giovanni looked to his side, the seat was empty. Campanella was gone. He jumped up and searched every corner of the carriage, calling his friend's name, but there was no answer. Outside, the Milky Way still shimmered, beautiful and vast. Giovanni pressed his face against the cold glass and whispered Campanella's name one more time, and his eyes stung with tears he tried not to let fall.

The conductor appeared then, a tall, gentle man in a deep blue uniform embroidered with silver stars. He glanced at the ticket in Giovanni's hand and his eyes widened just a little. Your ticket, he said, can take you anywhere in the galaxy. Your friend has reached the place he wanted to go. He was smiling when he left. The conductor placed a hand briefly on Giovanni's shoulder, and the touch felt like starlight, warm and impossibly kind.

Giovanni leaned back in his seat and closed his eyes. The train rocked him softly, its wheels tapping out a slow, steady rhythm on the rails, like a quiet lullaby. Behind his eyelids he saw Campanella's smile, the crystal pampas grass chiming in the wind, the golden apple's sweetness, the little girl waving goodbye. All of it wove together into something beautiful, a memory that felt more real than waking, a dream that would never quite fade.

When Giovanni opened his eyes again, he was lying in the grass on the hillside. The Milky Way still arched across the sky above him, and the festival lanterns flickered far below in the town. A cool night breeze carried the smell of river water and green grass. He sat up slowly. His heart felt full, as if someone had poured an entire galaxy of light inside it. He knew that Campanella was still somewhere out there, in the starlight, in the wind, in the space between heartbeats.

Giovanni walked slowly down the hill toward home. He wanted to tell his mother about the galaxy, the crystal fields, the golden apples, all of it. Moonlight lit his path, and every step he took seemed to land in a pool of silver. He looked up one last time at the Milky Way and thought he saw, far off in that river of light, a small train moving quietly among the stars. He smiled and whispered, good night, Campanella. Then he walked on, into the soft and gentle dark.
